slides.title: Introduction to Flutter & Dart
slides.content: Flutter, Google's open-source UI toolkit, enables developers to build high-performance, natively compiled applications for mobile, web, and desktop from a single codebase using the Dart language. Flutter leverages a reactive framework where UI components are 'widgets' arranged hierarchically; developers compositionally construct sophisticated user interfaces simply by assembling nested widgets that describe the visual state of the application. The Dart language, with its optional static typing and Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation during development, facilitates 'hot-reload,' a powerful feature permitting instantaneous UI updates as you write code, drastically increasing development velocity and interaction feedback. Furthermore, Flutter achieves native-level performance by compiling to machine code (Ahead-of-Time, or AOT) for release builds, circumventing the overhead traditionally associated with bridged technologies or hybrid web-view-based approaches. This makes it an ideal solution for teams wanting to maintain a single source of truth across mobile platforms without sacrificing performance, visual fidelity, or development speed in their application development landscape.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#f0f9ff'/><path d='M150 50 L250 50 L200 150 Z' fill='#0ea5e9' fill-opacity='0.6'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#0369a1'>Single Codebase, Multi-Platform</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How is the concept of 'Hot Reload' implemented and why is it useful?
Compare Flutter's performance to hybrid web-view approaches.
Explain the widget-centric architectural approach in Flutter.

slides.title: Android Lifecycle & Jetpack
slides.content: Building for the Android platform requires a deep understanding of the Application and Activity lifecycles, ensuring the app behaves predictably as it transitions between states—being created, paused, resumed, or destroyed based on system resource availability and user interaction. Jetpack, Android's powerful suite of libraries, simplifies navigating this complexity by providing components for lifecycle management, data binding, state management, and UI construction (Jetpack Compose). Jetpack Compose revolutionizes UI development on Android by shifting from traditional imperative XML layouts to a declarative, functional programming style that defines the visual component hierarchy purely through code. By embracing these modern libraries, Android developers are empowered to build cleaner, more testable, and more responsive interfaces that naturally adapt to various form factors and system lifecycle events. Understanding the lifecycle is the cornerstone of writing robust Android apps that avoid memory leaks, handle configuration changes (like screen rotation) elegantly, and react gracefully when pushed to the background, ensuring consistent user experiences across thousands of device types.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#ecfdf5'/><ellipse cx='200' cy='100' rx='100' ry='50' fill='#10b981' fill-opacity='0.2'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#065f46'>Android Lifecycle & Compose</text></svg>
studentPrompts: Why is understanding the Activity lifecycle critical to avoiding memory leaks in Android?
How does Jetpack Compose simplify the development of reactive UI components compared to XML layouts?
Describe how Jetpack Compose handles state propagation without the need for traditional data binding tools.
